topic: Someone discovers their partner has been writing a novel about them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The laptop screen glowed with a title that made my stomach clench: *The Cartography of Elara*. My breath hitched in my throat as I realized the sprawling narrative, the intricate character arcs, the painfully familiar descriptions of my morning routine, my silly habits, the way I always hum off-key while making coffee. It was all me. A novel. I slowly closed the lid, the soft click echoing loudly in the quiet living room. My hands were trembling, gripping the edge of the sofa cushion until my knuckles were white. When he finally walked in, whistling tunelessly, I didn't look up. I just stared at the floor, the silence between us thick and suffocating, heavy with unspoken accusations. "What is it?" he asked, his voice light, completely missing the seismic shift happening inside me. I managed a small, tight nod, my jaw clenched so hard I could feel the strain in my neck.

[story 2]

I found the folder tucked deep within his desktop files—a manuscript, meticulously formatted, titled simply, "My Subject." The air in the apartment suddenly felt thin, like I couldn't draw a full breath. Every word, every carefully constructed scene detailing my anxieties and triumphs, was laid bare before me. A hot flush crept up my neck, spreading across my cheeks. I wanted to scream, to demand an explanation, but the sound caught in my chest, trapped behind a sudden, desperate lump in my throat. I stood by the window, watching the city lights blur, feeling a profound hollowness settle over me. When he approached, tentative and concerned, I turned, my eyes glassy and wide. "You shouldn't have done that," I whispered, the sound barely audible, laced with a brittle edge that I immediately regretted.

[story 3]

It was the discovery of the annotated drafts that truly dismantled me. Hidden within the document were his marginalia—notes about my favorite sweater, the precise inflection of my laughter when I was genuinely surprised, the detailed description of the way I chewed my lip when concentrating. Reading it felt like being subjected to an intimate, unwanted dissection. I paced the length of the room, my steps quick and jerky, my gaze darting between the screen and his oblivious form across the room. My hands began to shake uncontrollably, and I pressed the heels of my palms against my eyes, trying to physically block the intrusive imagery. When he finally noticed my agitation, he stopped mid-sentence. The sudden stillness was jarring. I dropped my hands, the tension draining out of me only to be replaced by a heavy, sinking feeling in my chest, a dull ache that radiated outward. "This isn't fair," I managed, my voice sounding strangely distant, as if speaking from another room.
